questions.question: How many distinct TDS rate bands exist across Section 393's three tables?
questions.explanation: Seven distinct TDS rate bands: 0.1% (goods purchase/e-commerce), 1% (immovable property, VDA, rent-P&M, motor vehicle TCS), 2% (contractor-individual, rent-P&M, technical fees, most TCS), 5% (NR infra bonds, NR REIT), 10% (rent-land, professional, director, royalty, dividend, MF, partner salary), 20% (NR general, LRS other), 30% (winnings).
questions.explanationWrong: Seven bands spanning from 0.1% to 30%. Plus slab rates for salary under Section 392. Knowing which band a payment falls into is the foundation for correct TDS compliance.

questions.question: Which payment type attracts the lowest TDS rate of 0.1%?

options.text: Sale of goods by specified persons (old 194Q)

questions.explanation: Sale of goods (old Section 194Q) attracts TDS at 0.1% under Table 1, Sl. 1, with a threshold of Rs 50 lakh aggregate per year. This is the lowest non-zero TDS rate in the framework.
questions.explanationWrong: 0.1% applies to purchase of goods (old 194Q) under Table 1, Sl. 1. The threshold is Rs 50 lakh aggregate. This minimal rate creates an information trail without imposing a significant cash flow burden on buyers.

questions.question: Under the new Act, which single section governs all TCS provisions?
questions.explanation: Section 394 consolidates all TCS into a single table. This is a review from Unit 3.6 where TCS rates were rationalized to a uniform 2% for most entries.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 394 = TCS. Section 392 = salary TDS. Section 393 = non-salary TDS (3 tables). Section 398 = defaults and interest.

questions.question: Which payments fall in the 30% rate band — the highest TDS rate?

options.text: Lottery/crossword/card game winnings, online gaming winnings, and horse race winnings

questions.explanation: The 30% rate applies exclusively to winnings: Table 3, Sl. 1 (lottery/card games), Sl. 2 (online gaming), and Sl. 3 (horse race). These are the highest-taxed TDS items because winnings income allows no deduction for expenses.
questions.explanationWrong: Only winnings attract 30%. Cash withdrawals are at 2%. NR payments can be at 20% (or DTAA rates). Professional fees and royalty are at 10%. The 30% rate reflects the nature of winnings as windfall income.

questions.question: The 10% rate band covers the most entries. Which of these is NOT at 10%?
questions.explanation: Individual contractor payments are at 2% (Table 1, Sl. 6(i)), not 10%. Professional fees, dividend, mutual fund income, partner salary, rent on land/building, royalty, director fees — all are at 10%. Contractor payments split: individual at 2%, advertising at 10%.
questions.explanationWrong: Individual contractor TDS is 2%, not 10%. The 10% band includes: professional fees, royalty, dividend, interest on securities, rent-land/building, MF income, JDA, EPF, partner salary, and director fees.

questions.question: Which payments fall in the 1% TDS rate band?

options.text: Immovable property transfer, VDA transfer, and rent on plant & machinery

questions.explanation: The 1% band includes: immovable property transfer (Table 1, Sl. 2(i), old 194-IA), VDA transfer (Table 1, Sl. 3, old 194S), and rent on plant & machinery (Table 1, Sl. 7(i), old 194-I). Motor vehicle TCS is also at 1% (Section 394, Sl. 6).
questions.explanationWrong: Three TDS entries at 1%: property transfer, VDA, and rent-P&M. Plus motor vehicle TCS at 1%. Contractor payments are at 2% (individual) or 10% (advertising). Commission is at 5%.

questions.question: The 2% TDS rate covers diverse entries across multiple tables. Which of these is at 2%?
questions.explanation: E-commerce operator payments (old 194-O) are at 2% under Table 1, Sl. 8. Other 2% entries include: contractor-individual (Sl. 6(i)), rent by individual/HUF (Sl. 10), payment by individual/HUF to contractor (Sl. 11), lottery commission (Table 3, Sl. 4), and cash withdrawal (Table 3, Sl. 5).
questions.explanationWrong: E-commerce at 2%. Dividend is 10%. Lottery winnings are 30%. Professional fees are 10%. The 2% band spans Tables 1 and 3, covering several payment types.

questions.question: Under the new Act, what is the interest rate for failure to deposit TDS after deduction?
questions.explanation: 1.5% per month under Section 398(3)(a)(ii) for failure to deposit after deduction. Failure to deduct is lower at 1% per month. This is a review from Unit 3.7.
questions.explanationWrong: 1.5% for deposit failure. 1% for deduction failure. The higher rate for deposit failure reflects that the deductor has withheld the payee's money and is effectively using both parties' funds.

questions.question: Old Section 194C (contractor payments) maps to which entry in the new Act for individual/HUF contractors?
questions.explanation: Individual/HUF contractor payments map to Table 1, Sl. 6(i) at 2%. Company/firm contractors and advertising payments map to Sl. 6(ii) at 10%. The old 194C split between 1% and 2% has been restructured.
questions.explanationWrong: Individual contractors → Sl. 6(i) at 2%. The old 194C rate for individuals was 1% — so this is actually a rate increase from 1% to 2% in the new Act. Advertising/company contractors are at 10% under Sl. 6(ii).

questions.question: Which TCS entries are at 1% under the new Act?
questions.explanation: Only motor vehicle TCS remains at 1% (Section 394, Sl. 6, Rs 10 lakh threshold). All other goods entries that were at 1% (alcohol, scrap, coal) moved to 2% under Finance Bill 2026.
questions.explanationWrong: Motor vehicle is the sole 1% TCS entry. Alcohol, scrap, and coal all moved from 1% to 2%. Motor vehicle was deliberately excluded from the 2% rationalization.

questions.question: Which payments are in the 5% TDS rate band under Section 393?

options.text: Insurance commission, commission/brokerage, and rent on land & building/furniture

questions.explanation: The 5% band includes: insurance commission (Table 1, Sl. 4, old 194D), commission/brokerage (Table 1, Sl. 5, old 194H), and rent on land & building/furniture (Table 1, Sl. 7(ii), old 194-I). Some NR entries are also at 5% (infra bonds, REIT under Table 2).
questions.explanationWrong: Insurance commission, general commission, and rent-land/building are the Table 1 entries at 5%. Professional fees are at 10%. Lottery commission is at 2%. Cash withdrawal is at 2%.

questions.question: The 20% rate band applies to which types of payments?

options.text: All non-resident payments and all LRS remittances
NR general payments (where DTAA rate is higher or no DTAA), LRS other purposes, and immovable property consideration wholly in kind
Only partner salary above Rs 5 lakh

questions.explanation: The 20% band includes: NR payments under Table 2 (general rate), LRS other purposes TCS (Section 394, Sl. 7(b)), and immovable property transfer where consideration is wholly in kind (Table 1, Sl. 2(iii)). The NR 20% applies where there is no DTAA or the DTAA rate is higher.
questions.explanationWrong: 20% covers NR general, LRS-other, and property-in-kind. Not all NR payments are at 20% — some have lower DTAA rates. Not all LRS is at 20% — education/medical is 2%. The 20% band spans both TDS and TCS.

questions.question: Under the new Act, what form replaces old Form 26Q for filing non-salary TDS returns on residents?
questions.explanation: Form 140 replaces old 26Q for non-salary TDS quarterly returns on residents. This is a review from Unit 3.7's compliance framework.
questions.explanationWrong: Form 140 is the new 26Q. Form 138 replaces 24Q (salary). Form 144 replaces 27Q (non-residents). Form 143 replaces 27EQ (TCS).

questions.question: Old Section 194-IA for immovable property transfer maps to which serial number and what rate for standard cash consideration?
questions.explanation: Standard immovable property transfer (cash consideration) is Table 1, Sl. 2(i) at 1% with Rs 50 lakh threshold. Partly in kind is Sl. 2(ii) at 2%. Wholly in kind is Sl. 2(iii) at 20%. The rate escalates with the proportion of non-cash consideration.
questions.explanationWrong: Property transfer — cash = 1% (Sl. 2(i)), partly in kind = 2% (Sl. 2(ii)), wholly in kind = 20% (Sl. 2(iii)). The tiered approach based on consideration type is unique to immovable property transfers.

questions.question: The 10% rate band covers the most entries. Name three entries from different tables that share this rate.

options.text: Professional fees (Table 1, Sl. 9), NSS deposits (Table 3, Sl. 6), and partner salary (Table 3, Sl. 7)
Contractor-individual (Table 1, Sl. 6), cash withdrawal (Table 3, Sl. 5), and lottery commission (Table 3, Sl. 4)
Rent-P&M (Table 1, Sl. 7(i)), VDA (Table 1, Sl. 3), and online gaming (Table 3, Sl. 2)
Insurance commission (Table 1, Sl. 4), horse race (Table 3, Sl. 3), and goods purchase (Table 1, Sl. 1)

questions.explanation: Professional fees (Table 1, Sl. 9(i)) at 10%, NSS deposits (Table 3, Sl. 6) at 10%, and partner salary (Table 3, Sl. 7) at 10% — all share the 10% rate across Tables 1 and 3. Other 10% entries include dividend, MF income, rent-land, royalty, and interest on securities.
questions.explanationWrong: The correct trio spans Tables 1 and 3. Contractor-individual is 2%, cash withdrawal is 2%, lottery commission is 2%. Rent-P&M is 1%, VDA is 1%, online gaming is 30%. Insurance commission is 5%, horse race is 30%, goods purchase is 0.1%.
questions.newSection: Section 393, Table 1 Sl. 9 and Table 3 Sl. 6-7

questions.question: Old Section 194A (interest other than on securities) maps to which new Act reference?
questions.explanation: Old 194A maps to Table 1, Sl. 16 at 10%. The threshold is Rs 1,00,000 for senior citizens (increased from Rs 50,000 by Finance Act 2025) and Rs 40,000 for others. Interest on securities (old 193) is a separate entry at Table 1, Sl. 14.
questions.explanationWrong: 194A → Table 1, Sl. 16. Don't confuse with Sl. 14 (interest ON securities, old 193). 194A covers bank interest, FD interest, and similar — the most commonly encountered TDS provision for individuals.

questions.question: Old Section 194K (mutual fund income) maps to which new reference and at what rate?
questions.explanation: Old 194K maps to Table 1, Sl. 17 at 10% with Rs 10,000 threshold. This covers income distributed by mutual funds and UTI. The rate and threshold are unchanged from the old Act.
questions.explanationWrong: 194K → Table 1, Sl. 17 at 10%. Sl. 15 is dividend (old 194). Sl. 9 is professional fees (old 194J). Sl. 6 in Table 3 is NSS (old 194EE). Each has 10% but different serial numbers.

questions.question: TCS on overseas tour packages changed from a tiered 5%/20% structure to what under Finance Bill 2026?
questions.explanation: Overseas tour packages moved from tiered 5%/20% to a flat 2% with the threshold removed entirely. This is a review from Unit 3.6 — the most dramatic simplification in the TCS framework.
questions.explanationWrong: Flat 2%, no threshold. The old tiered structure (5% up to Rs 7 lakh, 20% above) was complex. The new flat 2% on all amounts is a massive simplification for tour operators.

questions.question: Old Section 194 (dividend) maps to which new reference, and what are the thresholds?

options.text: Table 1, Sl. 15 at 10%; Rs 10,000 for individuals, nil for others
Table 1, Sl. 14 at 10%; Rs 10,000 for all
Table 1, Sl. 13 at 10%; nil threshold for all
Table 3, Sl. 1 at 30%; Rs 10,000 for all

questions.explanation: Dividend (old 194) maps to Table 1, Sl. 15 at 10%. The threshold differs by payee type: Rs 10,000 for individuals, nil for others (companies, etc.). Deemed dividend (old 2(22)(e)/194) is a separate entry at Sl. 13.
questions.explanationWrong: Sl. 15 at 10%. The dual threshold is important: individuals get Rs 10,000 exemption, non-individuals get none. Sl. 14 is interest on securities, Sl. 13 is deemed dividend. All are at 10% but with different entries.

questions.question: Old Section 195 (non-resident payments) maps to which table in the new Act?
questions.explanation: Section 195 maps to Table 2 of Section 393. This table covers all payments to non-residents, with rates determined by the nature of income and applicable DTAA provisions. The general rate is 20%.
questions.explanationWrong: Table 2 = non-resident payments (old 195). The general NR rate is 20% where no specific provision or DTAA applies. Specific NR rates exist for interest, royalty, and technical fees under DTAA.

questions.question: What is the threshold for TDS on immovable property transfer (old 194-IA)?

options.text: No threshold — applies to all property sales

questions.explanation: Rs 50 lakh per transaction for immovable property transfer under Table 1, Sl. 2 (old 194-IA). If the sale consideration is below Rs 50 lakh, no TDS is required. The rate is 1% (cash), 2% (partly in kind), or 20% (wholly in kind).
questions.explanationWrong: Rs 50 lakh per transaction. This is a per-transaction threshold — each property sale is assessed independently. The threshold amount does not change based on the type of consideration (cash/kind).

questions.question: Contractor payments under old 194C have dual thresholds. What are they?

options.text: Rs 30,000 per single payment/contract AND Rs 1 lakh aggregate per year
Rs 50,000 per payment AND Rs 2 lakh aggregate
Rs 10,000 per payment AND Rs 50,000 aggregate
Rs 1 lakh per payment only — no aggregate threshold

questions.explanation: Contractor payments have a dual threshold: Rs 30,000 per single contract/payment OR Rs 1 lakh aggregate per year. TDS is triggered when either threshold is breached. Under the new Act, this maps to Table 1, Sl. 6.
questions.explanationWrong: Rs 30,000 per contract + Rs 1 lakh aggregate. The OR condition means even if no single payment exceeds Rs 30,000, TDS applies when aggregate payments to the same contractor cross Rs 1 lakh in the year.

questions.question: Under the new Act, Form 15G and Form 15H have been replaced by which single form?
questions.explanation: Form 121 merges both 15G and 15H, eliminating the age-based distinction. This is a review from Unit 3.7's compliance framework.
questions.explanationWrong: Form 121 replaces both. Form 128 is for lower withholding (old Form 13). Form 130 is the salary certificate (old Form 16). Form 133 is the consolidated certificate (old 16C/D/E/27D).

questions.question: Rent by specified persons (old 194-I) has a monthly threshold. What is it?
questions.explanation: Rs 50,000 per month is the threshold for rent TDS under Table 1, Sl. 7 (old 194-I). Both plant & machinery rent (1%) and land/building rent (5%) share this monthly threshold. The monthly threshold for individual/HUF rent payers (old 194-IB, Sl. 10) is also Rs 50,000.
questions.explanationWrong: Rs 50,000 per month. This applies to both types of rent under specified persons. The threshold was previously Rs 2.4 lakh per year — the new monthly expression is equivalent but changes how the threshold is tracked.

questions.question: What are the VDA (virtual digital asset) transfer thresholds under the new Act?

options.text: Rs 50,000 (specified person) / Rs 10,000 (others) aggregate per year
No threshold — applies from the first transaction

questions.explanation: VDA transfer (old 194S) has dual thresholds based on who the deductor is: Rs 50,000 aggregate per year if the deductor is a specified person, and Rs 10,000 aggregate per year for others. TDS rate is 1% under Table 1, Sl. 3.
questions.explanationWrong: Rs 50,000 for specified persons, Rs 10,000 for others. The lower threshold for non-specified persons ensures even P2P VDA transactions on smaller platforms are captured in the TDS net.

lessons.title: TCS Rate Comparison — Old vs New

questions.question: Summarize the TCS rate changes: how many entries increased, decreased, and remained unchanged?

options.text: 3 increased (alcohol, scrap, coal at 1%→2%), 4 decreased (tendu, timber, LRS-edu, overseas tour), 3 unchanged (motor vehicle, LRS-other, parking/toll)

questions.explanation: 3 increased: alcohol (1%→2%), scrap (1%→2%), coal (1%→2%). 4 decreased: tendu (5%→2%), timber (2.5%→2%), LRS-education (5%→2%), overseas tour (5%/20%→2%). 3 unchanged: motor vehicle (1%), LRS-other (20%), parking/toll (2%).
questions.explanationWrong: 3 up, 4 down, 3 same. The convergence pattern is clear: everything moved toward 2% from both directions, except motor vehicle (stayed at 1%), LRS-other (stayed at 20%, policy deterrent), and parking/toll (already at 2%).

questions.question: Which TCS entry saw the steepest rate DECREASE in absolute percentage points?

options.text: Tendu leaves — 5% to 2% (3 percentage point drop)
LRS education — 5% to 2% (3 percentage point drop)
Overseas tour — 20% to 2% (18 percentage point drop)

questions.explanation: Tendu leaves and LRS education both dropped 3 percentage points (5% to 2%). However, overseas tour's change is more nuanced — it went from a tiered 5%/20% to flat 2%, so the effective drop depends on the amount. Among single-rate entries, tendu and LRS-education tie at 3pp.
questions.explanationWrong: Tendu leaves and LRS education both dropped 3 percentage points. The overseas tour is complex because the old rate was tiered (5% up to Rs 7L, 20% above), so the effective decrease depends on the package value.

questions.question: What is the key change in the cash withdrawal TDS under the new Act compared to the old?

options.text: The 206AB higher rate for non-filers (5%) was removed — uniform 2% for all
The threshold was reduced from Rs 1 crore to Rs 50 lakh

questions.explanation: The removal of 206AB eliminated the 5% non-filer rate. Under the new Act, 2% applies uniformly regardless of return-filing status. This is a review from Unit 3.5.
questions.explanationWrong: 206AB removal is the key change. The 2% rate and Rs 1 crore threshold are unchanged. What changed is that non-filers no longer face a higher 5% rate — simplifying bank compliance significantly.

questions.question: Which NR-specific TDS entries are at 5% in Table 2?

options.text: Interest on infrastructure bonds and REIT/InvIT distributions to NRs
There are no 5% entries in Table 2

questions.explanation: Infrastructure bond interest (old Section 194LC) and REIT/InvIT distributions to NRs attract 5% TDS under Table 2. These are specific concessions for NR investments in Indian infrastructure and real estate.
questions.explanationWrong: NR infra bonds and REIT/InvIT at 5%. These concessional rates encourage foreign investment in infrastructure. The general NR rate is higher at 20%, making these 5% entries notable exceptions.

questions.question: For the 30% rate band, what distinguishes the threshold for online gaming (Sl. 2) from lottery (Sl. 1) and horse race (Sl. 3)?

options.text: Online gaming has a Rs 50,000 threshold
Online gaming has no monetary threshold — TDS applies on net winnings at withdrawal/year-end; lottery and horse race trigger at Rs 10,000
All three have the same Rs 10,000 threshold
Online gaming threshold is Rs 1 lakh

questions.explanation: Online gaming (Sl. 2) uses a net winnings concept with no fixed monetary threshold. TDS triggers at withdrawal or year-end. Lottery (Sl. 1) and horse race (Sl. 3) both have a Rs 10,000 per-event threshold. All share the 30% rate but differ in threshold mechanics.
questions.explanationWrong: No monetary threshold for online gaming. The net winnings approach (deposits minus withdrawals minus closing balance) replaces a fixed threshold, reflecting the continuous deposit-withdrawal cycle of gaming platforms.

questions.question: A company pays Rs 40,000 as professional fees to a resident CA. What TDS applies?
questions.explanation: Professional fees at 10% under Table 1, Sl. 9(i). Rs 40,000 exceeds the Rs 30,000 threshold. TDS = 10% of Rs 40,000 = Rs 4,000. The full amount is subject to TDS once the threshold is crossed.
questions.explanationWrong: 10% of Rs 40,000 = Rs 4,000. Professional fees are in the 10% band. The Rs 30,000 threshold is a trigger — once crossed, TDS applies on the entire payment, not just the excess.

questions.question: An individual pays Rs 55,000 monthly rent to a landlord. What is the TDS rate and which form is used?

options.text: 2% under Table 1, Sl. 10 (old 194-IB), filed via Form 141
5% under Table 1, Sl. 7 (old 194-I), filed via Form 140
10% under Table 1, Sl. 7(ii), filed via Form 138
No TDS — individuals are exempt from rent TDS

questions.explanation: Individual/HUF paying rent above Rs 50,000/month uses Table 1, Sl. 10 (old 194-IB) at 2%. The challan-cum-statement is Form 141 (old 26QC). Sl. 7 at 5%/10% applies to specified persons (companies, etc.), not individual payers.
questions.explanationWrong: 2% under Sl. 10, Form 141. Individual rent payers use a different provision (old 194-IB) than specified persons (old 194-I). The rate is lower (2% vs 5%/10%) and the compliance form is Form 141, not Form 140.

questions.question: Under the new Act's compliance framework, the quarterly return for non-resident TDS is filed using which form?
questions.explanation: Form 144 (old 27Q) for non-resident TDS returns. This is a review from Unit 3.7.
questions.explanationWrong: Form 144 = NR TDS return. Form 140 = resident non-salary. Form 143 = TCS. Form 138 = salary. The complete return form mapping: 24Q→138, 26Q→140, 27Q→144, 27EQ→143.

questions.question: A bank pays Rs 1,20,000 interest to a senior citizen resident (age 68). What TDS applies?

options.text: Rs 2,000 (10% of Rs 20,000 excess over Rs 1,00,000 senior citizen threshold)
Rs 8,000 (10% of Rs 80,000 excess over Rs 40,000 general threshold)
No TDS — senior citizens are exempt

questions.explanation: Senior citizens get a higher threshold of Rs 1,00,000 for interest TDS (Table 1, Sl. 16, old 194A) — increased from Rs 50,000 by Finance Act 2025. Excess = Rs 1,20,000 - Rs 1,00,000 = Rs 20,000. TDS = 10% of Rs 20,000 = Rs 2,000. The general threshold for non-seniors is Rs 40,000.
questions.explanationWrong: TDS = 10% of excess over Rs 1,00,000 (senior threshold, increased from Rs 50,000 by Finance Act 2025) = Rs 2,000. If this were a non-senior, the threshold would be Rs 40,000, yielding TDS of Rs 8,000. The enhanced senior citizen threshold saves Rs 6,000 in TDS.

questions.question: A company pays Rs 15,000 dividend to an individual shareholder. Is TDS applicable?

options.text: Yes — 10% on Rs 5,000 excess over Rs 10,000 threshold = Rs 500
No — dividends are exempt from TDS

questions.explanation: Dividend TDS under Table 1, Sl. 15 at 10%. Individual shareholders have a Rs 10,000 threshold. Excess = Rs 15,000 - Rs 10,000 = Rs 5,000. TDS = 10% of Rs 5,000 = Rs 500.
questions.explanationWrong: TDS on the excess over Rs 10,000 = Rs 500. The Rs 10,000 threshold applies only to individual shareholders. For a company receiving the same dividend, TDS would apply on the full Rs 15,000 (nil threshold for non-individuals).

questions.question: A company pays a resident contractor Rs 2,00,000 for civil work and Rs 50,000 for advertising services. What is the total TDS?

options.text: Rs 4,000 (2% on civil) + Rs 5,000 (10% on advertising) = Rs 9,000

questions.explanation: Civil work by individual contractor: Table 1, Sl. 6(i) at 2% = Rs 4,000. Advertising: Sl. 6(ii) at 10% = Rs 5,000. Total = Rs 9,000. Different contractor payment types have different rates within the same serial number.
questions.explanationWrong: Different rates for different contractor categories. Civil/transport/catering = 2% (for individuals). Advertising = 10%. You cannot combine them — each is computed separately at its applicable rate.

questions.question: A company rents a machine for Rs 60,000/month and office space for Rs 80,000/month from the same landlord. What are the TDS rates?

options.text: Machine: 1% (Sl. 7(i)); Office: 5% (Sl. 7(ii)) — different rates for different asset types
Both at 5% — rent is rent regardless of asset type

questions.explanation: Rent on plant & machinery is 1% under Sl. 7(i). Rent on land & building/furniture is 5% under Sl. 7(ii). Both exceed the Rs 50,000/month threshold. Machine TDS = Rs 600, Office TDS = Rs 4,000. Different asset types attract different rates within the same serial number.
questions.explanationWrong: Machine rent = 1%, office rent = 5%. The split within Sl. 7 reflects the old 194-I distinction. Machine rent at 1% (reduced from old 2%) is one of the few genuine rate changes in the new Act.

questions.question: Old Section 206AB imposed higher TDS for non-filers. What happened to this provision under the new Act?

options.text: It was entirely omitted — no higher rate for non-filers exists
The rate was increased to 10% for non-filers

questions.explanation: Section 206AB was entirely omitted from the 2025 Act. No higher TDS/TCS rate for non-filers exists in the new framework. This is a review from Units 3.5 and 3.6.
questions.explanationWrong: 206AB is gone completely. This eliminates the compliance burden of checking non-filer status before applying TDS rates. Uniform rates now apply regardless of the payee's return-filing history.

questions.question: A specified person pays Rs 2 lakh to a resident for professional fees and Rs 25,000 for commission/brokerage. What is the total TDS?

options.text: Rs 20,000 (10% on professional only; commission below threshold)

questions.explanation: Professional fees (Sl. 9(i)) at 10% on Rs 2,00,000 = Rs 20,000 (exceeds Rs 30,000 threshold). Commission (Sl. 5) at 5% on Rs 25,000 = Rs 1,250 (exceeds Rs 15,000 threshold). Total = Rs 21,250. Each entry has its own rate and threshold.
questions.explanationWrong: Rs 20,000 + Rs 1,250 = Rs 21,250. Professional fees at 10% (Sl. 9(i)) and commission at 5% (Sl. 5) are separate entries. You never blend rates — each is computed independently on its own amount.

questions.question: An NR receives Rs 10 lakh as technical service fees from an Indian company. No DTAA exists. What TDS rate applies?

options.text: 20% under Table 2 (NR general rate, no DTAA)

questions.explanation: NR payments without a DTAA attract the general 20% rate under Table 2. If a DTAA existed, the lower treaty rate would apply. Table 1 rates apply to residents. Table 3 is for 'any person' entries like winnings and cash withdrawals.
questions.explanationWrong: 20% under Table 2 for NR payments with no DTAA. The 10% Table 1 rate would apply if the payee were a resident. For NRs, Table 2 governs, and without a treaty, the domestic rate of 20% prevails.

questions.question: A company in a single month pays: (1) Rs 50,000 professional fees to a CA, (2) Rs 1,20,000 rent for office, (3) Rs 5,000 dividend to a shareholder. Calculate total TDS.

options.text: Rs 5,000 (professional) + Rs 6,000 (rent) + Rs 0 (dividend below Rs 10,000) = Rs 11,000

questions.explanation: Professional: 10% of Rs 50,000 = Rs 5,000. Rent-office: 5% of Rs 1,20,000 = Rs 6,000 (exceeds Rs 50,000/month). Dividend: Rs 5,000 is below Rs 10,000 threshold for individuals — no TDS. Total = Rs 11,000.
questions.explanationWrong: Rs 11,000 total. Professional at 10%, rent-office at 5%, and dividend below the Rs 10,000 individual threshold. Each payment type has its own rate and threshold — you must check each independently.

questions.question: Match old sections to rate bands: (A) 194-IA = ?, (B) 194H = ?, (C) 194B = ?, (D) 194A = ?
questions.explanation: 194-IA (property) = 1%, 194H (commission/brokerage) = 5%, 194B (lottery winnings) = 30%, 194A (interest) = 10%. These span four different rate bands, demonstrating the diversity of TDS rates across payment types.
questions.explanationWrong: A=1%, B=5%, C=30%, D=10%. Property at 1%, commission at 5%, winnings at 30%, interest at 10%. Each old section maps to a specific rate band in the new framework.

questions.question: Under the new Act, TDS on partner salary was introduced as old Section 194T. What rate and threshold apply?
questions.explanation: Partner salary/remuneration/interest: 10% above Rs 20,000 under Table 3, Sl. 7. Introduced in 2024 as the newest TDS provision. This is a review from Unit 3.5.
questions.explanationWrong: 10% above Rs 20,000. Section 194T (2024) covers salary, remuneration, commission, bonus, and interest paid by a firm to partners. It is the newest entry in the entire TDS framework.

questions.question: A business sells Rs 3 crore of coal and also buys Rs 60 lakh of goods from a vendor. What is the combined TDS/TCS obligation?

options.text: TCS on coal: Rs 6 lakh (2%); TDS on goods purchase: Rs 1,000 (0.1% on Rs 10 lakh excess over Rs 50 lakh)
TCS on coal: Rs 3 lakh (1%); TDS on goods purchase: Rs 600 (0.1%)
TCS on coal: Rs 6 lakh (2%); No TDS on goods — below threshold
TCS on coal: Rs 15 lakh (5%); TDS on goods: Rs 6,000 (1%)

questions.explanation: Coal TCS (Sl. 5): 2% of Rs 3 crore = Rs 6 lakh. Goods purchase TDS (Table 1, Sl. 1, old 194Q): 0.1% on excess over Rs 50 lakh = 0.1% of Rs 10 lakh = Rs 1,000. The business has both a TCS collection obligation (as seller) and a TDS deduction obligation (as buyer).
questions.explanationWrong: TCS = Rs 6 lakh (coal at 2%, old rate was 1%), TDS = Rs 1,000 (goods at 0.1% on excess over Rs 50 lakh). The coal rate doubled from 1% to 2%. Goods purchase TDS at 0.1% is the lowest rate in the framework.

questions.question: Rank these TDS rates from lowest to highest: professional fees, contractor-individual, lottery winnings, goods purchase, NR general.

options.text: Goods purchase (0.1%) < contractor-individual (2%) < professional fees (10%) < NR general (20%) < lottery winnings (30%)
Contractor-individual (1%) < goods purchase (2%) < professional fees (5%) < NR general (10%) < lottery winnings (30%)
Goods purchase (0.1%) < contractor-individual (1%) < NR general (10%) < professional fees (20%) < lottery winnings (30%)
All are at 10% except lottery at 30%

questions.explanation: The ranking spans all seven rate bands: 0.1% (goods purchase) < 2% (contractor-individual) < 10% (professional fees) < 20% (NR general) < 30% (lottery winnings). Understanding the rate hierarchy is essential for quick reference during compliance work.
questions.explanationWrong: 0.1% → 2% → 10% → 20% → 30%. This sequence touches five of the seven rate bands (missing 1% and 5%). Each rate band serves a different policy purpose — from information trail (0.1%) to deterrence (30%).
